subsystem: Qt GUI painting pipeline — QPaintEngineEx::stroke(), the extended paint-engine entry point that converts a vector path plus a pen into a filled outline. It reads the path's raw element-type and coordinate arrays, feeds them element-by-element through a QStroker/QDasher (which emit the stroked outline into a cached QStrokeHandler), then hands the resulting outline to fill(). It is the core of how every QPainter::drawPath / drawLine / drawPolygon stroke is rasterised.
provenance: Identified by the Qt-internal symbol vocabulary: the QPaintEngineEx class, the Q_D(QPaintEngineEx) private-data accessor macro, QVectorPath, QPainterPath::ElementType (MoveToElement/LineToElement/CurveToElement/CurveToDataElement), Qt::PenStyle/Qt::SolidLine/Qt::NoPen, QVectorPath::WindingFill / NonConvexShapeMask / CurvedShapeMask flag masks, and the qpaintengineex_moveTo/lineTo/cubicTo free-function hooks. The banner names the file corpus/train/train_0133_cwe787.cpp and the leading comment tags it 'primevul_test_0203_vuln'. This is the well-known qpaintengineex.cpp translation unit from Qt's src/gui/painting.
trust_boundary: The hostile input is the QVectorPath `path` argument (lines 85-89): path.elements() (the element-type stream), path.points() (the raw qreal coordinate stream) and path.elementCount() (the element count that derives every loop bound). Vector paths are routinely built from untrusted document content — SVG, PDF, PostScript, HTML/SVG in QtWebKit/WebEngine, or QDataStream-deserialised QPainterPath objects — so an attacker who can supply a document controls the element-type sequence, the number of coordinates actually present, and the elementCount. The pen (inPen) and its dash pattern are comparatively trusted, but the dash-pattern size is also attacker-influenceable through document styling. The critical invariant the caller cannot be trusted to uphold is that the element-type array and the coordinate array are mutually consistent (i.e. that a CurveToElement is genuinely backed by three following coordinate pairs and that elementCount coordinate pairs are actually present).

what_the_code_does.explanation: The entry point takes a const reference to the vector path to be stroked and the pen describing how to stroke it. An optional debug build prints the pen. Q_D(QPaintEngineEx) expands to a QPaintEngineExPrivate* d giving access to the engine's private state (the cached stroke handler, the stroker/dasher objects, device rects, and the currently selected active stroker). The first real guard is path.isEmpty(): a path that reports itself empty causes an immediate return. Note this guard trusts the path's own notion of emptiness; whether isEmpty() implies elementCount()==0 and a non-null points array is a contract the rest of the function silently depends on.

On the first stroke ever performed by this engine the StrokeHandler is lazily allocated with an initial capacity of path.elementCount()+4, and the stroker's three emission hooks are wired to the free functions qpaintengineex_moveTo/lineTo/cubicTo which push generated outline vertices into strokeHandler->pts and outline element types into strokeHandler->types. Crucially the handler is cached on the private object (d->strokeHandler) and is created exactly once — its initial sizing is derived from whatever the *first* path happened to contain, even though it will be reused for every later, possibly much larger, path. The '+4' is slop for close/join vertices.

A local mutable copy of the pen is made (pen). For dashed pens (style greater than SolidLine) the code computes a device-space clip rectangle and estimates how many dash periods would be drawn. It sums the dash pattern segment lengths, but caps the number of segments it will sum at 32 via qMin(pattern.size(), 32) and clamps each segment to be non-negative with qMax(..., 0). patternLength is multiplied by the pen width. If the total pattern length is effectively zero the pen is downgraded to NoPen (avoiding a divide-by-zero on the next branch, which is why line 41 must run before line 43). If the visible extent divided by the pattern length exceeds 10000 — i.e. the dashes would be sub-pixel and produce an unwieldy number of segments — the pen is collapsed to a semi-transparent solid line instead. This is purely a performance/rendering heuristic.

If the (possibly downgraded) pen differs from the last-used stroker pen, the stroker is reconfigured: join/cap/miter, and a stroke width (a zero width becomes cosmetic width 1). Then the active stroker is chosen by pen style: SolidLine uses the plain QStroker, NoPen selects a null stroker (nothing to draw), and any other style uses the QDasher configured with the dash pattern and offset. d->activeStroker is a raw pointer into d->stroker or d->dasher (or nullptr). Because this whole block is gated on the pen having changed, activeStroker retains its previous value across calls when the pen is unchanged — it is engine state, not a local.

If the active stroker is null (NoPen) there is nothing to stroke and the function returns. Otherwise the computed clip rect is pushed into the stroker (for dash clipping), and if the plain stroker is active its 'force open' flag is set from the path's explicit-open hint (whether the path's subpaths should be treated as open regardless of implicit close).

This is the heart of the parser and the source of every bound. `types` points at the path's element-type array (one QPainterPath::ElementType per element, or nullptr for a pure-polygon path where every element is an implicit LineTo). `points` points at the raw coordinate array, two qreals (x,y) per element. `pointCount = path.elementCount()` is the element count as reported by the path. lastPoint is computed as points + (pointCount<<1), i.e. points advanced by 2*pointCount qreals — the intended one-past-the-end sentinel of the coordinate array. The cached strokeHandler's output buffers are reset (logically cleared, capacity retained) ready to receive the generated outline. Every subsequent loop uses `points < lastPoint` as its termination condition and dereferences `types` in lock-step, so the correctness of all reads rests entirely on these two arrays being mutually consistent and on pointCount being an honest, non-overflowing count.

The flags describing the *output* stroke path are assembled: winding fill rule always, non-convex-shape hint when there are more than two elements (a triangle or fewer is assumed convex), and a curved-shape hint if round caps/joins will introduce curves. These flags are later passed into the constructed QVectorPath strokePath so the fill stage can pick an appropriate rasterisation strategy.

This is the non-cosmetic fast path. The stroker's curve-flattening threshold is set from the current transform and begin() attaches the output handler. If a type array is present the code walks the path element by element: MoveToElement and LineToElement each consume one coordinate pair (points += 2, ++types); CurveToElement consumes three coordinate pairs — it reads points[0]..points[5] for the two control points and end point — and advances points += 6 and types += 3 (the curve occupies one CurveToElement type slot followed by two CurveToDataElement slots). The loop's only termination test is `points < lastPoint`; the `types` pointer is advanced in parallel but is never independently bounded. After the loop, if the path has an implicit close, a closing line is drawn back to the very first point via path.points()[0]/[1].

When there is no type array (types == nullptr) the path is a pure polyline: the first coordinate pair is an unconditional moveTo, and every subsequent pair is a lineTo until points reaches lastPoint, with an optional implicit-close line. Note the first moveTo(points[0], points[1]) executes before the while guard is ever evaluated. After emission, end() finalises the outline; if nothing was produced the function bails. Otherwise a new QVectorPath is constructed directly over the strokeHandler's generated point buffer and type buffer (pts.data()/types.data(), length types.size()) and handed to fill() together with the pen's brush — this is what actually paints the stroke.

The cosmetic-pen branch must transform the input coordinates itself (a cosmetic pen has device-space width, so the outline is generated in device space). For a perspective (TxProject) transform it falls back to converting to a full QPainterPath and using strokePath. Otherwise it mirrors the non-cosmetic loop exactly, but reinterpret-casts the raw qreal array to QPointF (two qreals) and multiplies each point by the current matrix before emitting. The MoveTo/LineTo cases read one QPointF, the CurveToElement case reads ((const QPointF*)points)[0..2] — the same three-pair, points+=6 / types+=3 advance — and the same points<lastPoint-only loop bound applies. The type-punning cast (const QPointF*)points assumes the coordinate array is laid out and aligned as an array of QPointF.

For the cosmetic branch the generated outline (already in device space) is wrapped in a QVectorPath and filled with the identity transform: the code saves the current matrix, resets it to identity, notifies transformChanged(), adjusts a non-solid brush's transform so the pattern still tracks the original transform, fills, then restores the matrix and notifies again. This save/restore is why the cosmetic outline is generated pre-transformed above. The function then returns.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The loop's sole termination condition is `points < lastPoint`, but a CurveToElement consumes SIX qreals (points[0]..points[5]) and advances types by three, without ever checking that three coordinate pairs — or three type slots — actually remain. If a malicious path places a CurveToElement as (or near) its final element with fewer than three coordinate pairs left, cubicTo reads points[2]..points[5] past lastPoint (out-of-bounds read of the coordinate array), and `types += 3` walks the parallel `types` pointer past the end of the element-type array; the very next iteration dereferences `*types` out of bounds. Because the two arrays are independently sized by the path producer, `points < lastPoint` being true does NOT imply `types` is still in range: a path whose type array is shorter than pointCount/... implies (e.g. all MoveTo/LineTo consuming 2 points and 1 type each, versus a producer that under-supplied types) desyncs the two pointers and the `switch (*types)` reads freed/adjacent heap. Impact: out-of-bounds read of heap memory feeding curve math (information disclosure / crash), and with a crafted heap layout the mis-parsed data can be turned into a controlled-length outline. The invariant the implementation must uphold — and does not here — is that before reading a curve it must verify points+6 <= lastPoint and that types+3 stays within the element-type array, i.e. bound both arrays, not just `points`.

pointCount is a signed int taken directly from path.elementCount(). The sentinel is computed as points + (pointCount<<1). If an attacker supplies a path whose elementCount exceeds INT_MAX/2, the shift `pointCount<<1` overflows a signed int — undefined behaviour, and in practice produces a small or negative value. A negative or wrapped multiplier makes lastPoint point *before* `points` (or only slightly after it), so `points < lastPoint` is false immediately or the pointer arithmetic itself is UB, silently truncating parsing; conversely, on 64-bit builds where the real allocation is far smaller than the claimed count, an honest-but-huge pointCount that does not overflow the int makes lastPoint run far past the actual coordinate buffer, so the loops above read arbitrarily far out of bounds. The invariant: elementCount must be validated as non-negative and small enough that 2*elementCount*sizeof(qreal) cannot overflow and matches the real coordinate-buffer length before it is used to derive lastPoint.

In the no-type-array (pure polyline) branch the first coordinate pair is dereferenced unconditionally — moveTo(points[0], points[1]) runs before any `points < lastPoint` check. This is safe only if path.isEmpty() (line 10) guarantees at least one point is present whenever types is null. If a path can report non-empty yet carry zero coordinate pairs (e.g. elementCount()==0 but isEmpty() keyed on some other predicate, or a deserialised path with an empty points array), points == lastPoint and reading points[0]/points[1] is an out-of-bounds read on an empty/zero-length buffer. The identical unconditional first-read appears in the cosmetic branch at lines 204-206. The invariant: the empty-path guard must guarantee at least one coordinate pair before these unconditional reads, otherwise every zero-point non-empty path over-reads.

The implicit-close handling reads path.points()[0] and path.points()[1] directly, assuming the coordinate array has at least one point. This same pattern recurs at lines 146-147, at line 199 ( *((const QPointF *) path.points()) ), and at lines 212-213. If a crafted path sets the implicit-close flag but supplies an empty coordinate array, these are out-of-bounds reads of the first pair; the cosmetic variant at line 199 additionally reinterpret-casts a possibly-empty buffer to QPointF. The invariant: hasImplicitClose() must not be honoured unless at least one point exists; the code trusts the flag and the array independently.

The StrokeHandler (which owns the pts/types output buffers written by the qpaintengineex_moveTo/lineTo/cubicTo hooks) is allocated exactly once, lazily, sized to the FIRST path's elementCount()+4, and then cached on the engine private and reused for every later stroke of arbitrarily larger paths. This is a latent out-of-bounds-write contract that lives in the StrokeHandler/QDataBuffer implementation (file not provided): if those buffers are fixed-capacity (rather than auto-growing) the initial reserve is treated as a hard bound, and stroking a later path that generates more outline vertices than the first path's elementCount+4 overflows the pts/types buffers on write — a heap out-of-bounds write (CWE-787) matching the sample's cwe787 tag. Even if QDataBuffer grows, `elementCount()+4` (int) can overflow for a huge first path. The invariant: the output buffers must grow to fit the current path's generated outline, and the reserve must never be treated as a fixed capacity across the cached handler's lifetime; this must be confirmed in the StrokeHandler/QDataBuffer add() path.

The cosmetic branch repeats the same unbounded curve read as lines 112-131 but through a reinterpret_cast of the raw qreal array to QPointF: ((const QPointF*)points)[0..2] reads three QPointF (six qreals) with the loop again only guarded by `points < lastPoint`, so a trailing CurveToElement over-reads the coordinate and type arrays identically. Additionally the type-pun (const QPointF*)points assumes the coordinate buffer is aligned and laid out as an array of QPointF; a QVectorPath whose points buffer does not satisfy QPointF alignment yields undefined behaviour on strict-alignment targets. Same invariant as the primary curve finding: verify three pairs remain before the read; do not rely solely on the points sentinel.

summary: This is Qt's QPaintEngineEx::stroke(). The dominant risk is that every parsing loop bounds itself only by the coordinate sentinel `points < lastPoint` while consuming a variable number of coordinates per element (six for a curve) and advancing the parallel element-type pointer independently — so an attacker-controlled QVectorPath with a trailing/short CurveToElement, mismatched type/point arrays, an overflowing elementCount, or an empty-but-not-'isEmpty' points buffer drives out-of-bounds reads (CWE-125) of heap memory. A latent out-of-bounds write (CWE-787) also lurks in the once-allocated, first-path-sized cached StrokeHandler if its output buffers do not grow. Since vector paths are routinely built from untrusted documents (SVG/PDF/deserialised QPainterPath), this is a remotely reachable memory-safety surface.

reviewed_anchors.reason: path is the untrusted input carrying the hostile element/point/count fields driving all loops
the ONLY emptiness gate before unconditional first-point reads; if it does not imply >=1 point, downstream reads over-read (covered by the lines 139-141 finding)
cached, first-path-sized output buffer reused for larger later paths — latent OOB write / int overflow of the reserve
explicitly capped at 32 and each segment clamped non-negative; bounded, no overflow of a buffer
guarded by qFuzzyIsNull(patternLength) at line 41 which downgrades to NoPen, so no divide-by-zero reaches this branch
null activeStroker is explicitly checked and returns; pointer aliases engine-owned d->stroker/d->dasher, not attacker memory

reviewed_anchors.anchor: types = path.elements(); points = path.points(); pointCount = path.elementCount() (lines 85-87)
reviewed_anchors.reason: the three untrusted fields; their mutual consistency is assumed but never validated
signed int shift can overflow for huge elementCount and a dishonest count makes the sentinel exceed the real buffer — CWE-190/125

reviewed_anchors.anchor: primary parse loop `while (points < lastPoint) switch(*types)` with CurveToElement points[0..5], points+=6, types+=3 (lines 112-131)
reviewed_anchors.reason: loop bounds only `points`, not the 6-wide curve read nor the parallel types pointer — OOB read CWE-125
unconditional first-point read on a possibly-empty buffer when the close flag is set
first pair dereferenced before any bound check; over-reads a zero-length points buffer
constructed over engine-produced output buffers whose consistency is internal; not directly attacker-bounded (the write risk is captured by the StrokeHandler-sizing finding)

reviewed_anchors.anchor: cosmetic branch (const QPointF*)points curve reads (lines 184-188) and QPointF type-pun casts
reviewed_anchors.reason: same unbounded curve over-read as the primary loop plus an alignment/type-pun assumption on the raw coordinate buffer
delegates to a higher-level painter-path conversion; no raw pointer arithmetic in the shown code
state bookkeeping around fill; no untrusted-length or buffer contract

findings.title: Unbounded curve read: parse loop guards only `points`, not the 6-wide curve read or the parallel types pointer
findings.anchor: while (points < lastPoint) { switch (*types) ... case CurveToElement: cubicTo(points[0..5]); points += 6; types += 3; }
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read (variable-length element parse bounded only by a single pointer)
findings.invariant: Before reading a CurveToElement the loop must verify that three coordinate pairs remain (points + 6 <= lastPoint) AND that the parallel element-type pointer stays within the element-type array (types + 3 within bounds); the `types` array length and the `points` array length must be independently bounded, since one sentinel cannot bound the other.
findings.failure_mode: A malicious/deserialised QVectorPath whose final element is a CurveToElement backed by fewer than three coordinate pairs, or whose element-type array is shorter than its coordinate array implies, causes cubicTo to read points[2..5] past lastPoint and `types += 3` to walk `*types` past the type array; the next `switch (*types)` dereferences out-of-bounds heap. Result: heap over-read feeding curve flattening — crash or information disclosure, and a lever for further mis-parsing. Vector paths built from untrusted SVG/PDF/deserialised QPainterPath make this remotely reachable.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether QVectorPath guarantees elements()/points() are mutually consistent for all producers (including QDataStream deserialisation), or whether the loop must add explicit points+6<=lastPoint and types-in-range checks.

findings.title: Cached StrokeHandler sized to the FIRST path's elementCount+4 and reused for larger later paths
findings.bug_class: Heap out-of-bounds write (output buffer sized once, reused for larger input)
findings.invariant: The StrokeHandler pts/types output buffers must grow to hold the outline generated for the CURRENT path; the initial reserve of elementCount()+4 must be a hint, never a fixed capacity, and must not be treated as a bound for any subsequent, larger stroke performed through the cached handler. elementCount()+4 must also not overflow int.
findings.failure_mode: The handler is allocated lazily exactly once and cached on the engine private; if the first stroked path is small, the buffers are sized small, and a later path that generates more outline vertices than the initial reserve overflows the fixed-capacity buffers on write via the qpaintengineex_moveTo/lineTo/cubicTo hooks — a heap out-of-bounds write. Even with a growing QDataBuffer, elementCount()+4 can overflow to a small/negative reserve for a huge first path.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether StrokeHandler's pts/types (QDataBuffer) auto-grow on add() or treat the constructor reserve as a hard capacity, and whether the handler is ever re-sized for later, larger paths.

findings.title: lastPoint sentinel derived from signed `pointCount<<1` — integer overflow / oversized bound
findings.bug_class: Signed integer overflow / unvalidated count driving a pointer bound
findings.invariant: path.elementCount() must be validated non-negative and small enough that 2*elementCount*sizeof(qreal) cannot overflow, and must equal the real length of the points() buffer, before being used to compute the lastPoint sentinel.
findings.failure_mode: pointCount is a signed int from elementCount(); `pointCount<<1` is undefined behaviour and in practice wraps for elementCount > INT_MAX/2, producing a small/negative multiplier so lastPoint lands before/near `points` (silent truncation), while a large-but-honest count on 64-bit makes lastPoint run far past the real coordinate buffer so the parse loops read arbitrarily out of bounds. Attacker controls elementCount via the path.
findings.what_to_confirm: The type and provenance of elementCount(), and whether any caller validates it against the actual points() buffer length before stroke() runs.

findings.title: Unconditional first-point read when the type array is null (empty polyline)
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read (missing empty-buffer guard before unconditional dereference)
findings.invariant: The path.isEmpty() guard at line 10 must guarantee at least one coordinate pair whenever the type array is null; the no-type branch dereferences points[0]/points[1] (and the cosmetic mirror at lines 204-206) before any `points < lastPoint` check.
findings.failure_mode: A path that reports non-empty but carries a zero-length coordinate array (points == lastPoint) causes moveTo(points[0], points[1]) to read past the end of an empty/zero-sized buffer. Reachable if isEmpty() keys on something other than 'has at least one point', e.g. a deserialised or programmatically built path.
findings.what_to_confirm: Exactly what QVectorPath::isEmpty() tests, and whether a non-empty path can have zero coordinate pairs.

findings.title: Implicit-close reads first point without verifying the coordinate array is non-empty
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read (trusting a flag independently of buffer length)
findings.invariant: hasImplicitClose() must only be honoured when at least one coordinate pair exists; the flag and the points() buffer length are attacker-controlled independently. The same pattern recurs at lines 146-147, 199 and 212-213.
findings.failure_mode: A crafted path that sets the implicit-close flag but supplies an empty points array causes path.points()[0]/[1] to read out of bounds; the cosmetic variant at line 199 additionally reinterpret-casts the empty buffer to QPointF before reading.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether hasImplicitClose() can ever be true for a path with zero points, and whether points() returns a valid pointer for an empty path.

audit_checklist: In every stroke parse loop, add an explicit remaining-length check before reading a CurveToElement (points + 6 <= lastPoint) and bound the parallel `types` pointer against the element-type array end, not just `points < lastPoint`.
Confirm QVectorPath guarantees elements() and points() are mutually consistent for ALL producers, especially QDataStream-deserialised QPainterPath, or add validation before stroking.

Inspect StrokeHandler / QDataBuffer: verify pts/types buffers auto-grow on add() and that the constructor reserve (elementCount()+4) is never treated as a fixed capacity across the cached handler's reuse for larger paths.
Validate path.elementCount() as non-negative and bounded so that pointCount<<1 and 2*pointCount*sizeof(qreal) cannot overflow when computing lastPoint.

Confirm QVectorPath::isEmpty() implies at least one coordinate pair, so the unconditional moveTo(points[0],points[1]) in the null-types branch and the hasImplicitClose() first-point reads cannot over-read an empty buffer.
Check alignment/type-punning safety of (const QPointF *)points in the cosmetic branch on strict-alignment targets.
